result.markdown: --- title: "Lecture 12" from: markdown+emoji subtitle: "Dealing with many zeros" format: revealjs: margin: 0 logo: NTNU_UofG.png theme: uofg_theme.scss footer: "Back to Home" header-includes: | toc: true toc-depth: 1 toc-title: "" slide-number: "c/t" title-slide-attributes: # data-background-image: images/trondheim3.png data-background-size: cover data-background-opacity: "0.55" author: - name: Sara Martino #orcid: 0000-0002-6879-4412 email: sara.martino@ntnu.no affiliations: Dept. of Mathematical Science, NTNU - name: Janine Illian #orcid: 0000-0002-6879-4412 email: Janine.Illian@glasgow.ac.uk affiliations: University of Glasgow - name: Jafet Belmont #orcid: 0000-0002-6879-4412 email: Jafet.Belmont@glasgow.ac.uk affiliations: University of Glasgow # date: May 22, 2025 # bibliography: references.bib embed-resources: true execute: allow-html: true freeze: auto --- ::: {.cell} ::: ## Motivation ::: incremental - Count data are often modeled with **Poisson** or **Negative Binomial** models.\ - These assume zeros occur naturally from the count process. - Example: for Poisson is $P(Y=0) = \exp(\lambda)$ - But in real data, we often see **too many zeros** → *zero inflation*. - Examples: - Many people have **0 doctor visits** per year.\ - Most customers have **0 claims**.\ - Many species are **absent** from samples. ::: ## Two main solutions Both handle excess zeros but use **different assumptions** about how zeros are generated. - **Zero-Inflated** Models - **Hurdle** Models - (**Occurrence** Models) ## Two main solutions Both handle excess zeros but use **different assumptions** about how zeros are generated. - **Zero-Inflated** Models - Two sources of zero: inflation process or count process - **Hurdle** Models ## Two main solutions Both handle excess zeros but use **different assumptions** about how zeros are generated: - **Zero-Inflated** Models - Two sources of zero: inflation process or count process - **Hurdle** Models - One one source of zeros ## Zero-Inflated Models Assume two processes: - A binary process → decides if the observation is a certain zero. - A count process → generates counts, *including* zeros. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P}(Y=0) & = \pi + (1-\pi)f(0) & \\ \text{P}(Y=y) & = (1-\pi)f(y)& y=1,2,\dots \end{aligned} $$ . . . **Note** In zero-inflated models $f(y)$ is typically discrete (ex Poisson, Binomial, ...) ## Hurdle Models {.smaller} Also have two parts, but different logic: - A binary model determines if the observation crosses the hurdle (i.e. zero vs. positive). - A **truncated** count model models only positive counts. **Zeros come only from the first process**. $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P}(Y=0) & = \pi & \\ \text{P}(Y=y) & = \frac{f(y)}{1-f(0)}& y=1,2,\dots \end{aligned} $$ . . . **Note** In hurdle models $f_Y(y)$ can also be continuous (f.ex Gamma). In these cases: $$ \begin{aligned} \text{P}(Y=0) & = \pi & \\ f_Y(y) & = f(y)& y>0 \end{aligned} $$ where $f(y)$ is scaled so that it integrates to $1-\pi$ ## Comparison: Zero-Inflated vs. Hurdle Models | Feature | Zero-Inflated | Hurdle | |:-----------------------|:-----------------------|:-----------------------| | **Zeros come from** | Two sources (inflation + count) | One source (hurdle only) | | **Positive part** | Includes zeros | Truncated at zero | | **Can use continuous distributions?** | :x: Typically count only | :white_check_mark: Yes (Gamma, Lognormal) | ## Zero Inflated models in `inlabru` (Type 1) - Four zero-inflated models are implemented - Poisson (`zeroinflatedpoisson1`) - Binomial (`zeroinflatedbinomial1`) - Negative Binomial (`zeroinflatednbinomial1`) - BetaBinomial (`zeroinflatedbinomial1`) To get details about the distributions you can type ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} INLA::inla.doc("zero inflated") ``` ::: . . . The probability of the inflation process $\pi$ is a *hyperparameter* therefore cannot be modeled with covariates ## Example{.smaller} Number of fishes caught by fishermen at a state park. ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` nofish persons child count 1 1 1 0 0 2 0 1 0 0 3 0 1 0 0 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_Zero_Inflated_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-3-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Example: two models $$ \begin{eqnarray} \text{Model 1: }\ & Y\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\\ \text{Model 2: }\ & Y\sim \text{NegBinomial}(n,p)\\ \text{Linear predictor: }\ & \eta = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1 + \beta_2x_2 \end{eqnarray} $$ ## Example: two models {.smaller} $$ \begin{eqnarray} \text{Model 1: }\ & Y\sim \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\\ \text{Model 2: }\ & Y\sim \text{NegBinomial}(n,p)\\ \text{Linear predictor: }\ & \eta = \beta_0 + \beta_1x_1 + \beta_2x_2 \end{eqnarray} $$ **Negative Binomial distribution** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: $$ \text{Prob}(Y=y) = \frac{\Gamma(y+n)}{\Gamma(n)\Gamma(y+1)}p^n(1-p)^y $$ with $$ E(Y) = \mu = n\frac{1-p}{p} \qquad \text{Var}(Y) = \mu(1+\frac{\mu}{n}) $$ and linear predictor $\eta = log(\mu)$ ## Example: Implementation {.smaller auto-animate="true"} ::::: columns ::: {.column width="65%"} **The Model** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Model 1 }: y_i|\eta_i & \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{Pois}(\lambda(\eta_i))\\ \text{Model 2 }: y_i|\eta_i & \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{NegBin}(n(\eta_i),p(\eta_i))\\ \text{Linear Predictor }: \eta_i & = \color{red}{\boxed{\beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i}}} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: {.column width="35%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} zinb[1:3,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` nofish persons child count 1 1 1 0 0 2 0 1 0 0 3 0 1 0 0 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: **The code** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="1-7"} # define model components cmp = ~ Intercept(1) + fishing(nofish, model = "linear") + persons(persons, model = "linear") + child(child, model = "linear") # define model predictor formula = count ~ . # build the observation model # Poisson model lik_pois = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatedpoisson1", data = zinb) # Negative binomial model lik_nbin = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatednbinomial1", data = zinb) # fit the model fit_pois = bru(cmp, lik_pois) fit_nbin = bru(cmp, lik_nbin) ``` ::: ## Example: Implementation {.smaller auto-animate="true"} ::::: columns ::: {.column width="65%"} **The Model** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Model 1 }: \color{red}{\boxed{y_i|\eta_i}} & \color{red}{\boxed{\sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{Pois}(\lambda(\eta_i))}}\\ \text{Model 2 }: y_i|\eta_i & \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{NegBin}(n(\eta_i),p(\eta_i))\\ \text{Linear Predictor }: \eta_i & = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: {.column width="35%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} zinb[1:3,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` nofish persons child count 1 1 1 0 0 2 0 1 0 0 3 0 1 0 0 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: **The code** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="9-13,21"} # define model components cmp = ~ Intercept(1) + fishing(nofish, model = "linear") + persons(persons, model = "linear") + child(child, model = "linear") # define model predictor formula = count ~ . # build the observation model # Poisson model lik_pois = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatedpoisson1", data = zinb) # Negative binomial model lik_nbin = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatednbinomial1", data = zinb) # fit the model fit_pois = bru(cmp, lik_pois) fit_nbin = bru(cmp, lik_nbin) ``` ::: ## Example: Implementation {.smaller auto-animate="true"} ::::: columns ::: {.column width="65%"} **The Model** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Model 1 }: y_i|\eta_i & \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{Pois}(\lambda(\eta_i))\\ \text{Model 2 }: \color{red}{\boxed{y_i|\eta_i}} & \color{red}{\boxed{\sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{NegBin}(n(\eta_i),p(\eta_i))}}\\ \text{Linear Predictor }: \eta_i & = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: {.column width="35%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} zinb[1:3,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` nofish persons child count 1 1 1 0 0 2 0 1 0 0 3 0 1 0 0 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: **The code** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="15-18,22"} bru_options_set(control.compute = list(dic = T, waic = T, mlik = T)) # define model components cmp = ~ Intercept(1) + fishing(nofish, model = "linear") + persons(persons, model = "linear") + child(child, model = "linear") # define model predictor formula = count ~ . # build the observation model # Poisson model lik_pois = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatedpoisson1", data = zinb) # Negative binomial model lik_nbin = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatednbinomial1", data = zinb) # fit the model fit_pois = bru(cmp, lik_pois) fit_nbin = bru(cmp, lik_nbin) ``` ::: ## Results - Fixed effects ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Poisson Model" ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean sd 0.025quant 0.975quant Intercept 0.22 0.15 -0.08 0.51 fishing -0.90 0.12 -1.13 -0.67 persons 0.65 0.05 0.56 0.74 child -0.95 0.10 -1.15 -0.76 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Negative Binomial Model" ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean sd 0.025quant 0.975quant Intercept -0.80 0.31 -1.42 -0.18 fishing -0.59 0.25 -1.08 -0.10 persons 0.94 0.12 0.71 1.17 child -1.65 0.19 -2.03 -1.27 ``` ::: ::: ## Results - Hyperparameters and scores Hyperparameters ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Poisson Model" ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean sd zero-probability parameter for zero-inflated poisson_1 0.48 0.04 ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` [1] "Negative Binomial Model" ``` ::: ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean sd size for nbinomial_1 zero-inflated observations 0.56 0.10 zero-probability parameter for zero-inflated nbinomial_1 0.06 0.06 ``` ::: ::: Scores ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Score Poisson Nbin 1 DIC 1111.36 795.44 2 Mlik -579.97 -424.72 3 WAIC 1207.12 798.86 ``` ::: ::: ::: notes There seems to be more overdispersion than excess zero..the negative binomial model better fits the data estimateing a small excess of zero. ::: ## Comparing estimated distribution for a specific data point $$ \begin{eqnarray} \text{Model 1: }\ & P(Y=0) &= \pi + (1-\pi)\ \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\\ \text{Model 2: }\ & P(Y=0)&= \pi + (1-\pi)\ \text{NegBinom}(n,p)\\ \end{eqnarray} $$ We check the distribution for one person, non-fisher and no children ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_Zero_Inflated_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-14-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Comparing estimated distribution - Implementation ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} df_pred = data.frame(nofish = 0, persons = 1, child = 0) prob_pois = predict(fit_pois, df_pred, formula = ~ { pi0 <- zero_probability_parameter_for_zero_inflated_poisson_1 lambda = exp(Intercept + fishing + persons + child) yy = 0:20 list( prob0 = pi0 * c(1,rep(0,20)) + (1-pi0) * dpois(yy, lambda = lambda)) } ) prob_nbin = predict(fit_nbin, df_pred, formula = ~ { pi0 <- zero_probability_parameter_for_zero_inflated_nbinomial_1 size = exp(size_for_nbinomial_1_zero_inflated_observations) mu = exp(Intercept + fishing + persons + child) p = size/(size + mu) yy = 0:20 list(prob0 = pi0 * c(1,rep(0,20)) + (1-pi0) * dnbinom(yy,size = size, mu = mu)) }) ``` ::: ## Comparing estimated distribution - Implementation Note: To get the names to input in `predict` use the `bru_names()` function: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} inlabru::bru_names(fit_pois) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` Intercept "Intercept" fishing "fishing" persons "persons" child "child" zero-probability parameter for zero-inflated poisson_1 "zero_probability_parameter_for_zero_inflated_poisson_1" ``` ::: ::: ## Hurdle Models in `inlabru` There are two ways to implement hurdle models in `inlabru` - **Strategy 1** Use a modified version of the same distributions defined for the zero-inflated models - **Strategy 2** Use a "two-likelihood" trick ## Strategy 1 - Implemented models {.smaller} **Available models** (Type 0) - Poisson (`zeroinflatedpoisson0`) - Binomial (`zeroinflatedbinomial0`) - Negative Binomial (`zeroinflatednbinomial0`) - BetaBinomial (`zeroinflatedbinomial0`) . . . **Advantages** - It works exactly as the zero-inflated models - No need for extra coding . . . **Disadvantages** - Can only be used for the models that are already implemented - The probability of zero ($\pi$) is fixed and cannot have covariates ## Example Strategy 1: Implementation {.smaller auto-animate="true"} ::::: columns ::: {.column width="65%"} **The Model** $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Model 1 }: y_i|\eta_i & \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{TruncPois}(\lambda(\eta_i))\\ \text{Model 2 }: y_i|\eta_i & \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\text{TruncNegBin}(n(\eta_i),p(\eta_i))\\ \text{Linear Predictor }: \eta_i & = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_{1i} + \beta_2 x_{2i} \end{aligned} $$ ::: ::: {.column width="35%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} zinb[1:3,] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` nofish persons child count 1 1 1 0 0 2 0 1 0 0 3 0 1 0 0 ``` ::: ::: ::: ::::: **The code** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # define model components cmp = ~ Intercept(1) + fishing(nofish, model = "linear") + persons(persons, model = "linear") + child(child, model = "linear") # define model predictor formula = count ~ . # build the observation model # Poisson model lik_pois = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatedpoisson0", data = zinb) # Negative binomial model lik_nbin = bru_obs(formula, family = "zeroinflatednbinomial0", data = zinb) # fit the model fit_pois0 = bru(cmp, lik_pois) fit_nbin0 = bru(cmp, lik_nbin) ``` ::: ## Example Strategy 1: Results ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_Zero_Inflated_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-19-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Strategy 2 - Two-likelihood trick **Advantages** - More flexible model - Can model also the probability of zero $\pi$ - Can also be used for continuous distributions with mass at 0 . . . **Disadvantage** - Some more coding ## Hurdle models com The Hurdle Model handles this by splitting the data-generating process into two parts: 1. **Zero-hurdle component**: This part is a binary model that estimates the probability of a zero count versus a non-zero count. 2. **Positive component**: This part models the distribution of the positive (non-zero) values. . . . This idea can be used in `inlabru` by using two likelihood - A binomial likelihood to model the 0/1 process - A continuous **positive** density or a **truncated** count distribution for the positive part ## Example - Gamma-Hurdle model {.smaller} Daily precipitation in Parana state ::::: columns ::: {.column width="50%"} $$ z_{st} = \left\{ \begin{eqnarray} 0 & \text{ if } &\text{ no rain on day } t \text{ at station } s\\ 1 & \text{ if } &\text{ rain on day } t \text{ at station } s \end{eqnarray} \right. $$ $$ y_{st} = \left\{ \begin{eqnarray} \text{NA} \text{ if } && \text{ no rain on day } t \text{ at station } s\\ \text{rain amout} \text{ if } && \text{ rain on day } t \text{ at station } s \end{eqnarray} \right. $$ $$ \begin{eqnarray} z_{st} & \sim \text{Binom}(p_{st}, n_{st} = 1);\\ (y_{st}|z_{st}=1) &\sim\text{Gamma}(a,b) \\ E(y_{st}) &= \mu_{st} = a/b \end{eqnarray} $$ ::: ::: {.column width="50%"} ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_Zero_Inflated_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-20-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_Zero_Inflated_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-20-2.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ::: ::::: $$ \text{Where}: \qquad \begin{eqnarray} \eta_t^1 & = \text{logit}(p_t) & = \beta_0^B+u^B(s) \\ \eta_t^2 & = \log(\mu_t)& = \beta_0^G+u^G(s) \end{eqnarray} $$ ## Example - Gamma-Hurdle model{.smaller} **Implementation** 1. Create response for binary and Gamma process ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} df = df %>% mutate(z = ifelse(value==0,0,1), y = ifelse(value==0,NA, value),) ``` ::: 2. Define components, observation models and run ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} cmp = ~ -1 + Intercept_z(1) + Intercept_y(1) + space_z(geometry, model = spde) + space_y(geometry, model = spde) + local_z(station, model = "iid") lik_z = bru_obs(formula = z ~ Intercept_z + space_z + local_z, data = df %>% filter(time==1), family = "binomial") lik_y = bru_obs(formula = y ~ Intercept_y + space_y, data = df %>% filter(time==1), family = "gamma") fit = bru(cmp, lik_z, lik_y) ``` ::: ## Example - Gamma-Hurdle model **Results** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ::: {.cell-output-display} ![](slides_Zero_Inflated_files/figure-revealjs/unnamed-chunk-23-1.png){fig-align='center' width=60%} ::: ::: ## Example - Gamma-Hurdle model We can: - use other components in both linear predictors $\eta^1$ and $\eta^2$ - have shared components between the two linear predictor - use other positive-continuous distribution instead of the Gamma (for example log-Normal, Weibull, etc.) ## Example - Poisson- Hurdle model Let's look again at the fishes examples. We now want to include covariates also in the model for the zero probability: $$ \begin{eqnarray} P(Y = 0) = &\pi\\ P(Y=y) = & (1-\pi) \frac{f_Y(y)}{f_Y(0)},& \qquad y =1,2,\dots \end{eqnarray} $$ We use the same "trick" as before and we now have $$ \begin{eqnarray} z\sim\text{Binomial}(\pi,n); &\qquad \eta_1 = \text{logit}(\pi) = \beta^1X\\ z\sim\text{Truncated Poisson}(\lambda); &\qquad \eta_2 =\log(\lambda) = \beta^2X\\ \end{eqnarray} $$ In `inlabru` the **truncated Poisson distribution** is called `nzpoisson` ## Example - Poisson- Hurdle model{.smaller} **Implementation** 1. Define response for binary and truncated poisson process ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} zinb = zinb %>% mutate(z = ifelse(count==0,1,0), y = ifelse(count==0,NA, count)) ``` ::: 2. Define components, observation models and run ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} cmp = ~ -1 + Intercept_z(1) + Intercept_y(1) + nofish_z(nofish, model = "linear") + persons_z(persons, model = "linear") + nofish_y(nofish, model = "linear") + persons_y(persons, model = "linear") lik_z = bru_obs(formula = z~ Intercept_z + nofish_z + persons_z, data = zinb, family = "binomial") lik_y = bru_obs(formula = y~ Intercept_y + nofish_y + persons_y, data = zinb, family = "nzpoisson") fit = bru(cmp, lik_z, lik_y) ``` ::: ## Example - Poisson- Hurdle model **Results** ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} round(fit$summary.fixed,2) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout} ``` mean sd 0.025quant 0.5quant 0.975quant mode kld Intercept_z 0.70 0.33 0.04 0.70 1.35 0.70 0 nofish_z 0.23 0.28 -0.33 0.23 0.78 0.23 0 persons_z -0.18 0.12 -0.41 -0.18 0.04 -0.18 0 Intercept_y 0.35 0.15 0.05 0.35 0.65 0.35 0 nofish_y -0.83 0.12 -1.07 -0.83 -0.59 -0.83 0 persons_y 0.53 0.04 0.44 0.53 0.61 0.53 0 ``` ::: ::: ## Example: Binomial-Hurdle model - In `inlabru` there is no implementation for the *truncated binomial* (or negative binomial) distribution - We can "trick" `inlabru` by using one of the implemented **Type 0** models $$ y_i|\eta_i \sim \pi1_{(y=0)} + (1-\pi)\frac{f(y)}{f(0)} $$ with a *fixed* and small $\pi$ thus creating a *truncated* distribution! ## Example: Binomial-Hurdle model {.smaller} **Implementation** 1. Define response and the components ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # Define response for binary and truncated neg. binomial zinb = zinb %>% mutate(z = ifelse(count==0,1,0), y = ifelse(count==0,NA, count)) # define model components cmp = ~ -1 + Intercept_z(1) + Intercept_y(1) + fishing_z(nofish, model = "linear") + fishing_y(nofish, model = "linear") + persons_z(persons, model = "linear") + persons_y(persons, model = "linear") + child_z(child, model = "linear") + child_y(child, model = "linear") ``` ::: . . . 2. Build the likelihood for the binomial part ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} # build the observation model Poisson model lik_binom = bru_obs(z ~ Intercept_z + fishing_z + persons_z + child_z , family = "binomial", data = zinb) ``` ::: . . . 3. Define the type 0 Negbinomial with $\pi$ fixed to a small value ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code code-line-numbers="5-6"} # Negative binomial model lik_trunc_nbin = bru_obs(y ~ Intercept_y + fishing_y + persons_y + child_y , family = "zeroinflatednbinomial0", data = zinb, control.family = list(hyper = list(theta = list(initial = -20,fixed = TRUE)))) ``` ::: . . . 4. Run the model ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} fit_nbin = bru(cmp, lik_binom, lik_trunc_nbin) ``` ::: ## True and false zeros ![](figures/findwally1.png){fig-align="center"} ## True and false zeros ![](figures/findwally2.png){fig-align="center"} ## Occupancy models {.smaller} **Basic idea** Data contain information about two processes: - The species occupacy (biological process) - The detection (observation process) **Basic model form** - Biological process $$ Z_i\sim\text{Bernoulli}(\phi_i),\qquad i = 1,\dots, M\ \ (\text{Number of sites}) $$\ - Observation process $$ d_{ij}\ |Z_i=z_i\sim\text{Bernoulli}(z_i\ p_{ij}), \qquad j = 1,\dots, K_i\ \ (\text{Number of visits at site } i) $$ **Note** The model requires multiple visits per site to be identifiable ## The Occupancy model {.smaller} For a set of set of $M$ discrete sites, let the detection history of the $i$th site $\mathbf{d}_i= d_{i1},\ldots,d_{iK}$. The joint likelihood is then: $$ L(\psi,p|\mathbf{d}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{d}_M) = \underbrace{\left[\prod_{i=1}^{m} \phi_i \prod_j^{K_i} p_{ij}^{d_{ij}} (1-p_{ij})^{1-d_{ij}}\right]}_{\text{sites with at least one 1}}\underbrace{\prod_{i=m+1}^M\left[\phi_i\prod_j^{K_i}(1-p_{ij})+(1-\phi_i)\right]}_{\text{sites with only 0}} $$ If we assume that $p_{ij}=p_i$ and let $y_i = \sum_j\ d_{ij}$ then: $$ L(\psi,p|\mathbf{d}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{d}_M)=\prod_{i=1}^{M} \left[\phi_i\ p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{K_i-y_i}+ I_{y_i=0}(1-\phi_i)\right] $$ - This is almost a Binomial Zero Inflated Model (ZIP) but now we are often more interested in $\phi$ (probability of presence) than in $p$ (probabilisty of detection) - We often want to have some covariates in the detection probability - Can we use INLA to fit this model? ## The Occupancy model {.smaller} $$ L(\psi,p|\mathbf{d}_1,\ldots,\mathbf{d}_M)=\prod_{i=1}^{M} \left[\phi_i p_i^{y_i}(1-p_i)^{K_i-y_i}+ I_{y_i=0}(1-\phi_i)\right] $$ - LGM **if** we assume $\eta =g(p)$ **or** $\eta = g(\phi)$ Gaussian - $\phi$ or $p$ (not both!) can be linked to the linear predictor $\eta$ . . . - What does this mean? - We can have *complicated* (with random effects) model for *either* $p$ of $\phi$ - The other parameter can only have some (not too many) linear covariates as components . . . - The likelihood `0binomialS` allows for exactly that ## Occupancy models - Example Modelling the spatio-temporal distribution of the Red-eyed Vireo in the Hubbard Brook Experimental Forest, New Hampshire, USA $$ \begin{aligned} &\text{Occupancy}\\ z_{t}(s) & \sim \text{Bernoulli}(\phi_t(s))\\ \text{logit}(\phi_t(s)) & = \eta_t(s) = \text{fixed + random effects}\\ &\text{Detection}\\ y_{ti}(s)| z_{t}(s) & \sim \text{Binomial}(z_{t}(s) \times p_{ti}(s))\\ \text{logit}(p_{ti}(s)) & = \alpha_0 + \alpha_1\ \text{julian_day}+ \alpha_2\ \text{hour_of_the_day} \end{aligned} $$ ## Occupancy models - Example ![](figures/occ1.png){fig-align="center"} ## Occupancy models - Example ![](figures/occ2.png){fig-align="center"} ## Occupancy models - Example{.smaller} The code is a bit more complicated than usual....but ask for details if you are interested :grinning: ::: {.cell layout-align="center"} ```{.r .cell-code} data <- st_as_sf(data.frame(scale_elev = scale(input$Xocc[,1]), coord1 = input$coord[,1], coord2 = input$coord[,2]),coords = c("coord1", "coord2")) response_data <- list(Y = input$Y, Int_det = 1, n = 3, X = input$X) formula <- inla.mdata(cbind(Y, n),Int_det=Int_det, X=X) ~ 0 + Int_occ(1, prec.linear = 1) + scale_elev(scale_elev, prec.linear = 1) + spatialfield(geometry, model = spde) fit <- bru( formula, family = "0binomialS", data = data, response_data = response_data, control.family = list(control.link = list(model = "logit"))) ``` ::: ## Summary - Treating with many zero{.smaller} There are mainly two types of models to treat excess of zeros - Zero inflated models - Hurdle models - Occurrence models ## Summary - Treating with many zero{.smaller} There are mainly two types of models to treat excess of zeros - Zero inflated models - Zeros come from two sources - Can only be used for counts (Poisson, Binomial,...) - Four likelihood are implemented in `inlabru` (Type1) - The probability of zero-inflation is **constant** - Hurdle models - Occurrence models ## Summary - Treating with many zero{.smaller} There are mainly two types of models to treat excess of zeros - Zero inflated models - Hurdle models - Only one source of zeros - Positive values can be both discrete and continuous - Two ways to imlement in `inlabru` ## Summary - Treating with many zero{.smaller} There are mainly two types of models to treat excess of zeros - Zero inflated models - Hurdle models - Only one source of zeros - Positive values can be both discrete and continuous - Two ways to implement in `inlabru` 1. Use the implemented Type0 likelihoods\ - Probability of zero is constant 2. Use the "two-likelihood" approach - Probability of zero can be modelled - Occurrence models ## Summary - Treating with many zero{.smaller} There are mainly two types of models to treat excess of zeros - Zero inflated models - Hurdle models - Occurrence models - Allow for covariates in both occurrence and detection probability (but random effects only in one of them) - Requires multiple observations

include-after-body:
<script>
  // htmlwidgets need to know to resize themselves when slides are shown/hidden.
  // Fire the "slideenter" event (handled by htmlwidgets.js) when the current
  // slide changes (different for each slide format).
  (function () {
    // dispatch for htmlwidgets
    function fireSlideEnter() {
      const event = window.document.createEvent("Event");
      event.initEvent("slideenter", true, true);
      window.document.dispatchEvent(event);
    }

    function fireSlideChanged(previousSlide, currentSlide) {
      fireSlideEnter();

      // dispatch for shiny
      if (window.jQuery) {
        if (previousSlide) {
          window.jQuery(previousSlide).trigger("hidden");
        }
        if (currentSlide) {
          window.jQuery(currentSlide).trigger("shown");
        }
      }
    }

    // hookup for slidy
    if (window.w3c_slidy) {
      window.w3c_slidy.add_observer(function (slide_num) {
        // slide_num starts at position 1
        fireSlideChanged(null, w3c_slidy.slides[slide_num - 1]);
      });
    }

  })();
</script>
